channel.title: NYT > Obituaries
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Pat Steir, Painter of Luminous ‘Waterfalls,’ Dies at 87
item.description: Spilling paint onto canvas and letting it streak down as it pleased, she often said that her celebrated works painted themselves.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth
item.media:credit: Ike Edeani for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pat Steir in her studio in 2018, sitting in front of examples of her “Waterfalls” series, paintings born of ingenuity and accident.

item.title: Leon Radvinsky, 43, Dies; Built the Adult-Entertainment Giant OnlyFans
item.description: By leveraging social media and the influencer economy, he turned his website into a byword for online pornography in the 21st century.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: Tracy Kidder, Author of ‘The Soul of a New Machine,’ Dies at 80
item.description: A Pulitzer Prize-winning narrative journalist, he wrote deeply reported books that often focused on heroic goodness in people.
item.dc:creator: John Schwartz
item.category: Soul of a New Machine, The (Book)
Mountains Beyond Mountains: The Quest of Dr. Paul Farmer, a Man Who Would Cure the World (Book)
Rough Sleepers: Dr. Jim O'Connell's Urgent Mission to Bring Healing to Homeless People (Book)
item.media:credit: Fred R. Conrad/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tracy Kidder in 1999 in Northampton, Mass. He moved to rural Western Massachusetts after completing a creative writing master’s program at the University of Iowa.

item.title: Boubacar Ould Messaoud, Leader in an Antislavery Fight, Dies at 80
item.description: Despite being imprisoned and harassed, he helped found an organization to combat slavery in his West African nation of Mauritania.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Boubacar Messaoud in 2002. “In my estimation, eradicating slavery is my duty, and I would sacrifice my life for it,” he said.

item.title: Daphne Selfe, World’s Oldest Professional Model, Dies at 97
item.description: Starting in 1949, she had an on-and-off career that lasted for 75 years. But her biggest success came late in life, modeling for labels like Dolce & Gabbana.
item.dc:creator: Bonnie Wertheim
item.media:credit: Visual China Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Daphne Selfe in 2010. Her skin-care and diet regimen, she said, included “Nivea, broccoli and the odd glass of champagne.”

item.title: Heisuke Hironaka, Groundbreaking Mathematician, Is Dead at 94
item.description: A recipient of his profession’s prestigious Fields Medal, he devised an algorithm that helps solve mathematical “singularities.” It now permeates the field.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: The Asahi Shimbun, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Heisuke Hironaka in 1975, during a period when he was giving lectures and appearing on Japanese television.

item.title: Larry Reed, Master of Shadow Puppetry, Dies at 81
item.description: Entranced by traditional Balinese puppet theater, he developed a modern, multicultural version that he performed around the world.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: Michel Rolland, 78, Renowned but Polarizing Global Wine Maven, Dies
item.description: A consultant with more than 150 clients on five continents, he was hugely influential. Supporters said he was a genius. Critics called him formulaic.
item.dc:creator: Eric Asimov
item.media:credit: Owen Franken for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michel Rolland at Chateau Lascombes, near Bordeaux, France, in 2006. He was the archetypal flying winemaker, jetting from client to client, and the wineries that sought his counsel were among the world’s most renowned.

item.title: Kermit Gosnell, 85, Dies; Imprisoned Abortion Doctor Convicted of Murders
item.description: Philadelphia prosecutors in 2013 said he killed babies who had emerged alive during late-term abortions. The case became a rallying cry for anti-abortion activists.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Chip Taylor, Writer of ‘Wild Thing’ and Other Classic Rock Hits, Dies at 86
item.description: Mr. Taylor, the younger brother of the actor Jon Voight, found success as a songwriter for the likes of Janis Joplin and Juice Newton.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Chip Taylor in 2003. “I wasn’t sure about ‘Wild Thing’ at first,” Mr. Taylor recalled in an interview in 1999. “On the demo I told the sound engineer to turn off the lights in the studio, and I just sat on a stool and sang it like a blues guy would.”

item.title: Mike Vernon, Who Helped Spark the British Blues Boom, Dies at 81
item.description: He produced albums — by John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers, with Eric Clapton, and the early Fleetwood Mac — that defined 1960s blues rock. He also shepherded David Bowie’s debut album.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Mike Vernon in 1979. He produced albums for Britain’s powerhouse Decca label as well as for his own label, Blue Horizon.

item.title: Robert White, Tenor Renowned for Irish Ballads, Dies at 89
item.description: A performer from childhood, he became a versatile singer in the mold of the Irish superstar John McCormack, adept at both classical repertoire and traditional songs.
item.dc:creator: James Barron

item.title: Catherine T. Nolan, a State Assembly Power From Queens, Is Dead at 67
item.description: In nearly four decades in Albany, leading key committees as a Democrat, she was an early supporter of liberal causes like labor rights and abortion protections.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Catherine T. Nolan spoke during a session of the New York General Assembly in 2019. In accumulating influence, she pushed against the traditional male-dominated politics of Albany.

item.title: Brian Doherty, 57, Dies; Chronicled Libertarians and Other Outsiders
item.description: Fascinated by the fringes, he wrote a definitive history of libertarianism and books about underground comics and the Burning Man festival.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Augie Meyers, Pioneer of Tex-Mex Music, Dies at 85
item.description: His carnival-like swirls on the Vox organ helped define the sound of the border with groups like the Sir Douglas Quintet and the Texas Tornados.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Augie Meyers in 1983. His bright, syncopated riffs on the Vox organ functioned as the sonic engine of the Sir Douglas Quintet and set the model for countless groups to follow.

item.title: Lilias Folan, Who Brought Yoga to Middle America, Dies at 90
item.description: With her bright leotards and soothing, welcoming tone, she helped to demystify a discipline that many Americans in the 1970s viewed as a counterculture practice.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:credit: Duane Howell/The Denver Post, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ms. Folan in 1976. “I’ve always known I was put on this planet to do this sharing and this journey,” she said.

item.title: Lionel Jospin, Who Helped Set France on Socialist Path, Dies at 88
item.description: As prime minister beginning in 1997, he introduced a national 35-hour workweek and oversaw the replacement of the franc with the euro.
item.dc:creator: David A. Andelman
item.media:description: Lionel Jospin delivering a speech in Paris in 2002. Through his parents, who were socialists, Mr. Jospin said he learned “the consequences of social misery, and the desire to change all of that.”

item.title: Jeff Webb, Who Built a Competitive Cheerleading Empire, Dies at 76
item.description: Through Varsity Spirit, the company he established in 1974, he turned cheerleading into a multibillion-dollar juggernaut and exerted control over almost every aspect of it.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan

item.title: J. Michael Bishop, Nobel Prize Winner for Cancer Research, Dies at 90
item.description: He helped discover cancer-causing genes. Later, as chancellor of the University of California, San Francisco, he led a major expansion.
item.dc:creator: Delthia Ricks
item.media:description: J. Michael Bishop in his lab in 1982. He won a Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1989 and was the chancellor of the University of California, San Francisco, from 1998 to 2009.

item.title: Robert Mueller, Former FBI Director Who Led Trump Inquiry, Dies at 81
item.description: He imposed the most significant overhaul of the F.B.I. in its history. Later, after concluding that Russia had interfered in the 2016 election, he became a target of the president’s anger.
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:description: Robert S. Mueller III in 2019, while serving as special counsel investigating Russia’s attack on the 2016 presidential election.

item.title: Paul Brainerd Dies at 78; Pioneered Desktop Publishing With PageMaker
item.description: His software brought printing into the digital age, allowing users to stop manually splicing columns of text and graphics and instead create layouts on a virtual pasteboard.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: Umberto Bossi, Fiery Founder of Italian Separatist Party, Dies at 84
item.description: A charismatic populist, he channeled the anger of a disaffected North, arguing — in inflammatory oratory — for secession and railing against a tide of migrants.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: Umberto Bossi in 2008. He appealed to disaffected northerners who saw Italy’s Rome-centered, “colonialist” national government as having robbed them of their hard-earned money and their cultural identity.

item.title: Nicholas Brendon, Beloved Sidekick on ‘Buffy the Vampire Slayer,’ Dies at 54
item.description: He played the part of Xander Harris, one of Buffy’s closest friends, on the hit television show about a teenage girl who protects the world from monsters.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Nicholas Brendon, who played the lovable sidekick on the hit show “Buffy the Vampire Slayer,” has died.

item.title: David Botstein, Gene-Mapping Pioneer, Dies at 83
item.description: His method of locating genes in human DNA allowed researchers to find disease-causing genes, and later to map the entire, sprawling human genome.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata

item.title: Shigeaki Mori, 88, Dogged Hiroshima Survivor Embraced by Obama, Dies
item.description: He lived through the first atomic bombing in Japan and then spent decades researching the identities of 12 American P.O.W.s killed in the attack.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Shigeaki Mori with President Barack Obama in 2016. The first American president to visit Hiroshima, Mr. Obama paid tribute to Mr. Mori, “a man who sought out families of Americans killed here, because he believed their loss was equal to his own.”

item.title: Bernard Rands, Pulitzer-Winning Composer, Dies at 92
item.description: His early successes in the 1960s, writing in the complex vein of high modernism, yielded later in his career to a more accessibly lyrical style.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Evening Standard/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Rands in 1971. He evolved into a composer whose music was compared by critics to that of Debussy, with shimmering orchestral textures and hints of tonality.

item.title: Chuck Norris, Action Star of ‘Walker, Texas Ranger,’ Dies at 86
item.description: He channeled his martial arts skills into heroic roles in films like “The Delta Force” and “Missing in Action” and in the long-running TV series “Walker, Texas Ranger.”
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:description: Chuck Norris in 1995 as Cordell Walker on “Walker, Texas Ranger.” The series aired on CBS from 1993 to 2001.

item.title: Calvin Tomkins, Who Profiled Giants of Modern Art, Dies at 100
item.description: On the staff of The New Yorker for more than 60 years, he wrote about Duchamp, Rauschenberg and many others. His books include “Living Well Is the Best Revenge.”
item.dc:creator: William Grimes
item.media:credit: Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
item.media:description: Calvin Tomkins at his home in Manhattan. A longtime staff writer at The New Yorker, he specialized in writing profiles of artists.

item.title: Patriarch Ilia II, a Spiritual Symbol of Stability in Georgia, Dies at 93
item.description: The longest-serving leader in the history of the Georgian Orthodox Church, he helped guide his country in its transition from Soviet repression to modern statehood.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: Patriarch Ilia II before a Christmas service in Tblisi, Georgia’s capital, in January 2008. He provided a sense of continuity and stability during tense periods in the country after the collapse of the Soviet Union.

item.title: Thomas Gentille, Artist Who Made Wearable Sculpture, Dies at 89
item.description: He was a master jeweler, but his pieces looked more like miniature contemporary artworks than anything you’d find at Cartier.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:description: Thomas Gentille in 1980. “I think you can express everything in jewelry,” he said. “And that’s what I’m trying to do.”

item.title: Jane Lapotaire, 81, Actress Hailed Across the Atlantic for ‘Piaf,’ Dies
item.description: A distinguished British stage veteran, she won an Olivier award in London and a Tony in New York for her dramatic portrayal of the French singer Édith Piaf.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Jane Lapotaire in “Piaf” in a 1979 London production. She later made her Broadway debut in the role. Frank Rich wrote in The Times that her performance “burns with such heart-stopping intensity that one never questions her right to stand in for ‘the little sparrow.’”
